It will yield concrete metrics for assessing democratic risks outlined in Coeckelbergh's prior work, moving from philosophical critique to actionable, data-driven analysis of relational norms in socio-technical systems.
proposals.novelty: While digital humanities tools are established, their application to systematically compare AI-shaped versus human-shaped narrative structures at scale is novel. It integrates computational text analysis with a relational, normative philosophical framework to move beyond analyzing single texts or models to mapping the emergent narrative ecology.

proposals.title: Relational Ontologies in Flux: A Systems-Theoretic and Democratic Inquiry into AI that Challenges Human-Machine Distinctions
proposals.motivation: The rapid development of large language models (LLMs) and embodied AI systems, pre-2026, increasingly destabilizes assumed ontological and ethical boundaries between humans and machines. This blurring creates urgent political and epistemic challenges for democracy, as seen in debates about AI agency, misinformation, and moral patiency, yet current ethical frameworks remain largely anchored in a priori distinctions. A systematic investigation is needed to understand the implications of this ontological erosion for human self-understanding and social organization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that AI systems designed with specific relational and emergent properties—such as long-term memory, narrative self-modeling, and participatory goal-setting—will catalyze a shift in human perception from seeing machines as tools to recognizing them as quasi-participants in social practices, thereby rendering traditional ontological demarcations pragmatically obsolete in specific contexts.
proposals.method: We will employ a three-phase, interdisciplinary method. First, we will technically implement a prototype multi-agent AI system using pre-2026 architectures (e.g., a transformer-based LLM with a persistent memory module and a reinforcement learning agent with a learned world-model) designed to exhibit behavioral continuity and narrative coherence. Second, we will embed this system in controlled, longitudinal participatory forums (inspired by democratic deliberation methods) where diverse human participants collaborate with it on complex tasks. Third, we will use qualitative discourse analysis and relational ethics frameworks to analyze the evolving norms, language, and justifications participants use to describe the system's role.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, empirically-grounded 'relational ontology' framework for human-AI interaction, moving beyond abstract philosophical debate. It will provide concrete evidence for how socio-technical systems reshape fundamental categories, offering critical insights for policymakers and ethicists on the preconditions for democratic coexistence in a world of increasingly ambiguous agents.
proposals.novelty: While existing work critiques anthropomorphism or defends clear boundaries, this proposal's novelty lies in its proactive, systems-based engineering of AI to *induce* ontological ambiguity, combined with a democratic, practice-oriented study of the resulting normative shifts, directly testing Coeckelbergh's relational norms in a novel empirical setting.

proposals.title: From Entrenchment to Correction: A Relational Framework for Bias-Mitigating Predictive Policing
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 critiques of predictive policing, such as those by Richardson et al. (2019) and Ferguson (2017), demonstrate that these tools often perpetuate and amplify systemic racial and socioeconomic biases present in historical crime data. This creates a profound ethical and political crisis, undermining public trust and democratic ideals of justice. The challenge is not merely to avoid harm but to explore if AI can be designed to actively identify and correct entrenched human biases within law enforcement systems.
proposals.hypothesis: By embedding a relational, system-level analysis of police-citizen interactions into the design of predictive tools, it is possible to create AI systems that flag potential instances of systemic bias in enforcement patterns, thereby reducing rather than entrenching discriminatory outcomes.
proposals.method: The research will employ a mixed-method, co-design approach. First, we will analyze historical policing data using fairness-aware machine learning algorithms (e.g., adversarial debiasing or reject option classification) to identify bias patterns. Second, drawing on Coeckelbergh's relational norms, we will develop a participatory framework involving police officers, community representatives, and ethicists to define normative constraints for the system. These constraints will be formalized into a feedback mechanism that alerts to potential bias in real-time predictions, prompting human review and systemic correction.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a novel, ethically-grounded technical framework for 'bias-correcting' AI in law enforcement, moving beyond critique to proactive design. It advances the field of AI ethics by operationalizing relational and democratic norms into concrete system architectures. The project will yield a proof-of-concept model and a set of procedural guidelines for human-AI cooperation in high-stakes public sectors.
proposals.novelty: While most pre-2026 work focuses on detecting or avoiding bias replication, this proposal actively designs AI as a corrective tool within the socio-technical system of policing. It uniquely integrates Coeckelbergh's relational philosophy with algorithmic fairness techniques to create a normatively guided, rather than purely statistically driven, intervention.

proposals.title: Relational Augmentation: Designing AI Systems for Epistemic Partnership Without Delegation
proposals.motivation: Current AI governance debates are dominated by a paradigm of delegation, where AI systems are framed as autonomous agents to which human decision-making authority is transferred. This framing, evident in discussions of AI ethics and regulation pre-2026, risks eroding human epistemic responsibility and democratic accountability. It creates a false dichotomy between full human control and full AI autonomy, neglecting the potential for systems that actively sustain and enhance human judgment.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly designing AI systems to avoid the delegation paradigm—through architectures that enforce human epistemic engagement and responsibility—we can create more trustworthy, democratic, and ethically robust human-AI collaborations. This relational approach will outperform delegation-centric models on metrics of user understanding, critical oversight, and shared epistemic ownership.
proposals.method: We will develop and test a prototype 'Epistemic Partner Interface' (EPI) for a complex judgment task (e.g., policy impact assessment). The EPI will integrate specific technical components: 1) A contrastive explanation generator (building on LIME or SHAP) that presents multiple, competing causal narratives, 2) A 'deliberation scaffold' that structures human input through Socratic questioning protocols before providing AI synthesis, and 3) A reasoning trace visualizer that exposes the system's uncertainty and inference paths. We will conduct controlled user studies comparing the EPI to a standard recommendation/delegation system, measuring outcomes like decision justification depth, over-reliance, and perceived co-authorship of the final judgment.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete technical and philosophical framework for 'non-delegative AI,' moving beyond abstract ethical principles to implementable system design. It will contribute to the fields of human-computer interaction and AI ethics by demonstrating how specific algorithmic choices can either undermine or support human epistemic agency and democratic norms.
proposals.novelty: While decision-support systems exist, this proposal uniquely rejects the delegation assumption as a foundational design constraint, integrating insights from Coeckelbergh's relational norms and democratic risk analysis into the core architecture. It shifts the focus from making AI explainable for auditing to making it dialogical for partnership.

proposals.title: Governing the Intelligence Ceiling: A Relational Systems Model for Socio-Technical Pathways that Prevent an Intelligence Explosion
proposals.motivation: The dominant narrative in AI futures, particularly in transhumanist and longtermist circles, frames the technological singularity as an inevitable, quasi-natural event. This deterministic framing preemptively narrows democratic deliberation and ethical governance, sidelining crucial questions about the desirability of such a future. It creates a political and epistemic risk by foreclosing alternative pathways where advanced AI serves human relational and democratic values without triggering an uncontrollable intelligence explosion.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling AI development as a complex socio-technical system governed by relational norms and democratic feedback loops, we can identify concrete governance and design interventions that make an intelligence explosion not just preventable, but actively undesirable from a societal perspective.
proposals.method: We will employ a systems dynamics approach, building a computational model that integrates technical factors (e.g., algorithmic efficiency, hardware scaling) with Coeckelbergh's relational norms (e.g., care, cooperation, epistemic humility) and democratic mechanisms (e.g., participatory technology assessment, value-sensitive design). The model will simulate multiple pathways, using agent-based modeling to test how different governance 'levers' (e.g., regulatory sandboxes, ethical licensing, public oversight boards) influence the trajectory of AI capability growth, prioritizing stability and human-AI cooperation over unbounded optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a counter-narrative and a practical modeling toolkit for policymakers and ethicists, moving beyond abstract risk debates. It will contribute to the field of AI ethics by operationalizing relational and democratic theories into testable system parameters, and to futures studies by demonstrating viable, desirable non-singularity scenarios.
proposals.novelty: While existing critiques of the singularity are often philosophical, this project uniquely combines relational ethics, democratic theory, and systems dynamics modeling to generate empirically-grounded, governable alternatives. It shifts the question from 'how to control the explosion' to 'how to design systems where the explosion is neither necessary nor wanted'.

proposals.title: Relational Governance for Systemic Bias: A Democratic Audit Framework for the AI Data Science Pipeline
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on algorithmic bias predominantly focuses on isolated technical fixes for model fairness, neglecting the socio-technical systems and power structures that embed bias from data collection through to deployment. This fragmented approach fails to address the relational and political dimensions of harm, undermining democratic values and epistemic justice in automated decision-making systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A relational, systems-oriented audit framework that maps and intervenes in the entire data science pipeline—from data conception to operational impact—will more effectively identify and mitigate systemic bias than stage-specific technical audits, by exposing the normative choices and power relations constitutive of each stage.
proposals.method: The research will develop and apply a mixed-methods framework. First, we will map a pipeline (e.g., for a public-sector predictive tool) using system dynamics to model feedback loops of bias. Second, we will integrate technical audits (using pre-2026 tools like AI Fairness 360 for statistical disparities and LIME/SHAP for explicability) with deliberative workshops involving affected communities, data scientists, and policymakers. This co-design process will surface relational norms and power asymmetries, translating them into concrete design requirements for each pipeline stage.
proposals.contribution: The project will contribute a novel, actionable audit methodology that bridges technical fairness metrics and democratic, relational ethics. It will produce a typology of 'relational bias' emergent in socio-technical systems and offer governance guidelines for cooperative human-AI development that prioritizes epistemic justice.
proposals.novelty: While existing work audits models or data in isolation, this framework uniquely treats the entire pipeline as a relational political system, integrating deliberative democratic methods with technical audit tools to govern the co-constitutive relationship between social norms and algorithmic bias.

proposals.title: Democratic Transparency: A Relational Framework for Explainable AI in Public Decision Systems
proposals.motivation: The deployment of AI in public decision-making (e.g., social services, urban planning) creates a democratic deficit when 'black-box' systems obscure the rationale for impactful decisions. Pre-2026, XAI techniques often focus on technical interpretability for experts, failing to address the epistemic and political needs of diverse stakeholders—citizens, caseworkers, and regulators—for meaningful understanding within their specific relational contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: That AI transparency can be made democratically meaningful by designing explanation systems that are dynamically adapted to the relational norms and epistemic positions of different human agents within a cooperative socio-technical system.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-tiered technical framework. First, a meta-model will classify stakeholder roles (e.g., affected citizen, auditing regulator) and map them to relational norms (from Coeckelbergh's work) like accountability or contestability. Second, this model will configure a modular XAI backend, selecting and tailoring explanation methods (e.g., LIME for local feature importance, counterfactual generators like DiCE, or concept activation vectors) to generate role-specific explanations. The system will be prototyped and evaluated using a simulated public allocation task with participatory design workshops.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a novel, philosophically-grounded technical architecture that operationalizes relational ethics for XAI. It moves the field beyond one-size-fits-all explanations by providing a method to generate context-sensitive transparency, thereby supporting human-AI cooperation and democratic oversight in public institutions.
proposals.novelty: While existing XAI research optimizes for algorithmic fidelity or user comprehension, this project uniquely integrates a normative, relational model of human-AI interaction directly into the technical design of the explanation system itself, making democratic values a constitutive engineering constraint.

proposals.title: Deconstructing the Modern Prometheus: A Narrative Analysis of AI Creation Myths and Their Ethical-Political Implications
proposals.motivation: Public and expert discourse on AI is saturated with powerful, often unexamined, narratives like the 'Frankenstein' myth, which frame AI as a runaway creation threatening its creator. These narratives, grounded in pre-2026 discourse, shape ethical priorities, policy debates, and public trust in technology, often obscuring relational and systemic realities. A rigorous analysis is needed to understand how these stories influence the conceptual landscape of AI governance and ethics.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying literary-critical narrative analysis to seminal AI creation myths will reveal embedded assumptions about agency, responsibility, and human-AI relations that constrain contemporary ethical and political thinking. We hypothesize that these narratives predominantly promote individualist, control-oriented ethical frameworks, marginalizing relational and care-based approaches.
proposals.method: This qualitative study will employ established methods from literary criticism and narrative theory. First, we will conduct a close reading and deconstruction of key texts (e.g., Shelley's *Frankenstein*, Čapek's *R.U.R.*) and their contemporary invocations in AI ethics literature. Second, using a systems-thinking lens, we will map the narrative elements (plot, character, metaphor) onto dominant ethical and political discourses in AI. The analysis will identify recurring tropes, such as the 'singular creator' and 'rebellious artifact,' to critique their implications for democratic deliberation and relational norms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a critical vocabulary for diagnosing the narrative underpinnings of AI ethics, moving debate beyond surface-level analogies. It will contribute to Mark Coeckelbergh's broader project of developing relational and democratic approaches to AI by exposing and challenging the limiting stories that currently shape the field.
proposals.novelty: While AI ethics frequently uses literary analogies, a systematic application of narrative theory as a primary analytical tool is novel. This approach moves beyond citing *Frankenstein* as a casual reference to treating it as a structured narrative system with specific, debatable ethical consequences.

proposals.title: Democratizing AI Ethics: A Relational Framework for Participatory AI System Design
proposals.motivation: Current AI ethics, as of 2025, is often criticized as being abstract, top-down, and disconnected from the lived experiences of those impacted by AI systems. This leads to principles that fail to translate into equitable and transparent technical practices. The risks to democratic discourse and social justice from opaque, non-participatory AI development are well-documented in pre-2026 literature on AI and democracy.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying structured participatory design (PD) methods from Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) to the AI development lifecycle will yield a concrete framework of relational norms that, when implemented, measurably increase stakeholder-perceived fairness and system transparency.
proposals.method: The research will employ a multi-stage, iterative participatory design process. We will adapt established HCI methods—such as cooperative prototyping, scenario-based design workshops, and laddering interviews—to engage diverse stakeholders (e.g., end-users, community advocates, frontline workers) in co-designing a specific high-stakes AI system, such as a municipal resource allocation tool. The process will be structured to elicit and formalize 'relational norms' for human-AI cooperation, which will then be translated into explicit system requirements and constraints. These will be implemented and tested in a simulated environment using explainability toolkits (e.g., SHAP, LIME) and fairness metrics (e.g., demographic parity, equalized odds) to assess the technical realization of co-designed norms.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, actionable methodology for bridging the gap between high-level AI ethics and technical implementation. It will produce a validated, relational framework of co-designed norms for AI, advancing the field beyond abstract principle lists towards democratically grounded, procedural ethics in AI system design.
proposals.novelty: While participatory design is established in HCI, its systematic application to the core ethical architecture of AI systems—moving beyond user interface concerns to shape fundamental system logic and fairness constraints—remains underexplored. This project uniquely integrates Coeckelbergh's relational ethics with concrete PD methods to operationalize democratic values in AI.

proposals.title: Relational Privacy by Design: A Democratic Systems Approach to AI as a Proactive Privacy Guardian
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm in AI development treats user data as a resource for extraction and targeting, creating systemic risks for autonomy, democracy, and human dignity. This project addresses a critical gap: the lack of AI systems explicitly designed with the primary, proactive function of protecting user privacy. Grounded in pre-2026 concerns about surveillance capitalism and algorithmic manipulation, it seeks to invert the standard logic of data-hungry AI.
proposals.hypothesis: That AI systems can be effectively architected as active privacy guardians by embedding relational norms of cooperation and care into their operational logic, and that such systems can be co-designed through democratic processes to ensure they align with diverse human values and contextual needs.
proposals.method: We will employ a participatory, systems-level methodology. First, we will conduct deliberative workshops with diverse stakeholders to define context-specific 'relational privacy norms'. Technically, we will prototype an AI agent architecture that uses reinforcement learning with a reward function shaped by these norms, prioritizing actions that minimize data exposure (e.g., local differential privacy, data minimization heuristics) while maintaining utility. The system will be evaluated in simulated digital environments against adversarial data extraction models.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a novel, ethically-grounded framework for 'privacy-by-design' AI, moving beyond compliance to proactive guardianship. It advances the field of human-AI cooperation by operationalizing relational and care ethics into technical systems, and offers a concrete model for democratically aligning AI development with fundamental human rights.
proposals.novelty: While privacy-enhancing technologies (PETs) exist, the proposal is novel in framing the AI itself as an autonomous, relational agent whose primary purpose is cooperative privacy protection, and in its integration of democratic co-design directly into the AI's normative architecture.

proposals.title: Designing for Human Flourishing: A Relational Framework for AI-Driven Economic Systems that Generate Meaningful Human Labor
proposals.motivation: The dominant narrative of AI-driven automation frames human labor as a cost to be minimized, risking widespread economic displacement and a crisis of meaning. This proposal addresses a critical gap in AI ethics and political economy: the lack of positive, systematic frameworks for designing AI systems that actively create, rather than eliminate, forms of work centered on uniquely human capacities for creativity, care, and judgment. Grounded in pre-2026 discourse on AI's impact on democracy and welfare, it seeks to move beyond risk mitigation to proactive institutional design.
proposals.hypothesis: By applying a relational, norm-driven systems analysis to economic design, we can identify specific configurations of AI tools and institutional rules that structurally incentivize and valorize new forms of 'human-only' creative and cooperative labor, thereby addressing the dual problem of economic viability and human flourishing.
proposals.method: The research employs a three-phase, interdisciplinary method. First, a conceptual analysis will synthesize Coeckelbergh's relational norms with economic theories of institutional design (e.g., Ostrom's principles) to create an evaluative framework. Second, this framework will be used to critically analyze and model existing pre-2026 socio-technical systems (e.g., platform co-ops, participatory budgeting platforms, creative commons ecosystems) as case studies. Third, we will conduct participatory workshops with stakeholders (workers, designers, policymakers) to iteratively design and refine speculative economic models, using system dynamics modeling to simulate labor market and well-being outcomes under different AI integration rules.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, normative framework for 'pro-human' AI economic design, moving the field from abstract ethical principles to actionable institutional blueprints. It will provide concrete, evaluated models for how AI can be governed to create, not just displace, meaningful work, offering a critical alternative to both fully automated luxury communism and surveillance capitalism.
proposals.novelty: While most pre-2026 work focuses on AI's risks to jobs or on universal basic income as a response, this proposal uniquely posits AI as a potential architect of new human-centric labor markets. It innovatively combines relational AI ethics with institutional economics to generate positive design principles, a synthesis absent from the contemporary literature.

proposals.title: Relational Ethics in Action: A Democratic Systems Approach to Safeguarding Autonomous Therapeutic Robots
proposals.motivation: The deployment of fully autonomous therapeutic robots raises profound ethical risks, including the erosion of genuine human care, opaque decision-making in sensitive contexts, and a potential shift in responsibility away from human institutions. Pre-2026 discourse, including Coeckelbergh's own work, highlights these dangers but often remains at the level of normative critique or abstract framework, lacking concrete technical pathways for implementation that align with relational and democratic values.
proposals.hypothesis: That a multi-layered ethical architecture, integrating formal deontic logic for rule-based safety, a consequentialist model for outcome evaluation, and a relational norm module trained on human-robot interaction corpora, can generate more contextually appropriate and trustworthy autonomous behavior than single-paradigm or purely statistical safeguards.
proposals.method: We will design a hybrid, modular reasoning system. A symbolic layer, using Answer Set Programming (ASP), will encode inviolable ethical rules (e.g., 'do not deceive'). A probabilistic layer, using Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes (POMDPs), will model long-term therapeutic outcomes. Crucially, a 'relational norm' module, built on a transformer architecture fine-tuned on annotated human-therapist dialogue and human-AI cooperation studies, will guide interaction style. These modules will be integrated via a meta-reasoner, with its arbitration logic co-designed through deliberative workshops with therapists, patients, and ethicists.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a technically detailed, implementable model for relational ethics in AI, moving from principle to practice. It will contribute a novel, hybrid safeguard architecture to the AI safety community and offer a concrete case study for democratic design in sensitive AI applications, bridging the gap between ethical philosophy and robotics engineering.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its systematic integration of Coeckelbergh's relational norms into a core AI reasoning architecture and its methodological commitment to shaping the system's arbitration mechanism through democratic deliberation, rather than purely top-down engineering or bottom-up learning.

proposals.title: Mapping the Legal-Ethical Gap: A Relational Framework for Minimally Untrustworthy AI Systems
proposals.motivation: The rapid deployment of AI systems, particularly large language models (LLMs), is governed by emerging legal frameworks like the EU AI Act, which focus on concrete risks and compliance. However, a significant gap exists between legal permissibility and ethical trustworthiness or social robustness. This creates a dangerous category of systems that are technically legal but ethically dubious, undermining democratic discourse and cooperative human-AI relations, as highlighted in pre-2026 debates on AI and democracy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a class of 'minimally untrustworthy' AI systems can be formally characterized by their adherence to legal thresholds while systematically violating key relational norms—such as transparency, accountability, and care for living beings—leading to predictable social harms not captured by law.
proposals.method: The research employs a mixed-method, systems-oriented approach. First, we will conduct a conceptual analysis using Coeckelbergh's relational norms framework to define core dimensions of untrustworthiness (e.g., epistemic opacity, moral displacement). Second, we will develop formal models using agent-based simulation (e.g., NetLogo) to test how systems operating at legal minima propagate misinformation or erode cooperation in simulated social networks. Third, we will analyze case studies of pre-2026 AI incidents (e.g., manipulative chatbots, biased hiring tools) to ground the formal models in documented evidence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel conceptual and analytical toolkit for policymakers and ethicists to identify and regulate AI systems in the legal-ethical gap. It will advance the field of AI ethics by moving from abstract principles to a testable model of relational failure, directly contributing to debates on AI's impact on democracy and human welfare.
proposals.novelty: While existing work critiques AI ethics or analyzes legal compliance, this project uniquely integrates relational philosophy with computational social science to formally model the boundary where legal AI becomes socially untrustworthy, offering a proactive diagnostic tool.

proposals.title: Deconstructing the Subject: Agency and Consciousness in AI Ethics
proposals.motivation: AI ethics debates increasingly hinge on contested metaphysical assumptions, particularly regarding AI agency and consciousness. These concepts, often imported uncritically from philosophy of mind and action theory, structure core discussions on moral patiency, responsibility, and rights. A rigorous analysis of their underlying binary oppositions (e.g., real/simulated, genuine/ersatz) is urgently needed to prevent conceptual confusion from derailing ethical and policy frameworks.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying deconstructive analysis to the foundational concepts of 'agency' and 'consciousness' in AI ethics will reveal inherent conceptual instabilities and power dynamics. This will demonstrate how these terms function to privilege certain ethical conclusions (e.g., against AI welfare) while marginalizing relational and practice-oriented approaches.
proposals.method: The project employs a philosophical, non-computational method of deconstructive textual analysis, informed by the work of Jacques Derrida and contemporary critical theory. It will systematically analyze key texts in AI ethics (2015-2025) that centrally deploy 'agency' or 'consciousness.' The technique involves identifying and tracing the hierarchical binary oppositions structuring these arguments (e.g., authentic/inauthentic experience, biological/digital), showing how the privileged term depends on its suppressed counterpart, thereby destabilizing the argument's conceptual foundation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a critical meta-ethical framework for AI ethics, moving debates beyond stalemates caused by undefined or essentialist terms. It will directly contribute to Mark Coeckelbergh's relational ethics project by undermining atomistic, property-based conceptions of moral status and shifting focus toward the dynamics of human-AI relations and socio-technical practices.
proposals.novelty: While deconstruction has been applied in technology studies, its systematic application to the core metaphysical assumptions of contemporary AI ethics is novel. It moves beyond cataloging risks or proposing principles to interrogate the very language and conceptual architecture of the field.

proposals.title: Democratic Nudges for Epistemic Health: A Relational Framework for Designing AI Interfaces that Promote Critical Thinking
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the widespread adoption of LLMs and personalized AI systems poses significant risks to democratic discourse by creating epistemic bubbles and reinforcing cognitive biases. This threatens the shared informational foundation necessary for democratic deliberation, a core concern in Coeckelbergh's work on AI and democracy. Addressing this requires moving beyond technical fixes to consider the human-AI relationship and the political implications of interface design.
proposals.hypothesis: That carefully designed digital nudges, grounded in a relational and democratic ethical framework, can be integrated into AI interface architectures to measurably improve user behaviors related to critical thinking and diverse information exposure without compromising autonomy.
proposals.method: The research will employ a systems-oriented, mixed-methods approach. First, we will conduct a conceptual analysis to derive relational norms (e.g., reciprocity, attentional fairness) for human-AI cooperation. Second, we will prototype a text-based AI assistant interface incorporating specific nudging algorithms: a) a diversification engine using content-based filtering and graph exploration to suggest counter-viewpoints, and b) a reflective prompt system that uses rule-based triggers to encourage source evaluation before presenting answers. These prototypes will be tested in controlled, scenario-based user studies measuring engagement diversity and cognitive reflection.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a novel, ethically-grounded design framework for 'democratic nudging' in AI, bridging behavioral economics and relational ethics. It provides concrete, testable interface mechanisms to operationalize epistemic values like critical thinking, directly addressing the political impact of AI system design on public discourse.
proposals.novelty: While digital nudging and algorithmic diversification exist, applying them through Coeckelbergh's specific lens of relational norms and democratic epistemology to AI interface design is novel. It shifts the focus from welfare or pure accuracy to fostering epistemic virtues within a cooperative human-AI system.

proposals.title: Freedom as Non-Domination in Algorithmic Governance: A Structural Analysis of AI in Climate, Finance, and Criminal Justice
proposals.motivation: The deployment of AI systems in high-stakes societal domains like climate, finance, and criminal justice increasingly structures human agency and social relations. Pre-2026 ethical discourse often focuses on individual bias or transparency, neglecting the broader political-structural question of how these systems create new relations of power and dependency. A systematic analysis of freedom, understood as non-domination, is urgently needed to evaluate whether AI governance architectures reinforce or mitigate arbitrary power over citizens.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying the republican political philosophy concept of freedom as non-domination reveals that current AI systems in climate, finance, and criminal justice domains create distinct, structurally embedded forms of arbitrary power, which can be diagnosed and mitigated through specific institutional and design interventions.
proposals.method: The research employs a conceptual-philosophical analysis grounded in political theory (drawing on Pettit and others) combined with a systems-oriented, domain-specific case study methodology. For each domain (e.g., climate AI for resource allocation, algorithmic trading and credit scoring, risk assessment tools in justice), we will: 1) map the key human-AI and institution-AI relations; 2) analyze these relations through the lens of non-domination, identifying sources of arbitrary power (e.g., opacity, dependency, lack of recourse); and 3) derive normative, relational principles for system design and regulatory frameworks that institutionalize contestability and control.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified political-structural framework for diagnosing threats to freedom across diverse AI applications, moving beyond individual-centric ethics. It will yield a set of domain-tested, relational norms for human-AI cooperation that prioritize institutional safeguards against domination, contributing directly to policy debates on AI governance and democratic control.
proposals.novelty: While non-domination has been discussed in general AI ethics, a systematic, comparative application to concrete, high-impact domains like climate and finance is lacking. This approach uniquely bridges republican political theory with the technical and institutional specifics of operational AI systems.

proposals.title: Institutional Logics and Power Dynamics: A Political Analysis of Robotic Integration in European Healthcare Systems
proposals.motivation: The integration of robots in healthcare, from surgical assistants to care companions, is accelerating, yet its governance is often framed narrowly in terms of safety and ethics. This overlooks how robots become embedded within, and reshape, existing institutional power structures, professional hierarchies, and resource allocation in healthcare systems. A political-institutional analysis is crucial to anticipate and democratically govern the systemic impacts on care quality, labor, and equity.
proposals.hypothesis: The integration of robots in healthcare is not a neutral technical process but is mediated and shaped by competing institutional logics (e.g., medical professionalism, managerial efficiency, technological solutionism), leading to distinct governance patterns and power shifts that can either reinforce or challenge existing inequalities.
proposals.method: This qualitative study will apply Elinor Ostrom's Institutional Analysis and Development (IAD) framework and concepts of institutional logics from political sociology. We will conduct a comparative case study of robotic integration in hospital systems in two European countries (e.g., Sweden and Germany). Data collection will involve document analysis of policy and procurement records, supplemented by semi-structured interviews with stakeholders (clinicians, administrators, policymakers, patients) to map the 'action arena,' relevant rules, and contested logics. Thematic analysis will identify key governance dilemmas and power dynamics.
proposals.contribution: The project will contribute a novel political-institutional lens to the ethics of healthcare robotics, moving beyond individual interaction models to systemic analysis. It will generate a typology of governance dilemmas specific to human-robot cooperation in institutional settings, offering concrete insights for designing more democratic and equitable integration policies.
proposals.novelty: While existing research examines human-robot interaction or ethical principles, this work uniquely applies established political science frameworks (IAD, institutional logics) to the empirical context of healthcare robotics, analyzing robots as institutional actors that reconfigure power and governance.

proposals.title: Mapping Power Asymmetries in Robotic Development: A Social Network Analysis of Stakeholder Influence and Democratic Risks
proposals.motivation: The development and deployment of robots and AI systems are shaped by complex networks of actors, yet the power dynamics within these networks remain opaque. This opacity poses significant risks to democratic values, epistemic justice, and equitable impact, as identified in pre-2026 critiques of AI governance. A systematic analysis is needed to move from abstract concerns about 'tech giants' to a concrete, empirical mapping of influence flows and potential domination.
proposals.hypothesis: Social network analysis will reveal that influence and resource flows in robotics development are concentrated in a small, non-diverse set of corporate and state actors, creating structural dependencies that marginalize end-users, civil society, and alternative epistemic perspectives, thereby embedding undemocratic norms into sociotechnical systems.
proposals.method: The research will employ mixed-methods Social Network Analysis (SNA). Data will be collected from public sources (funding databases, patent filings, consortium memberships, co-authorship networks) to construct multi-modal networks of actors (developers, institutions, policymakers). Centrality measures (e.g., degree, betweenness, eigenvector centrality) will quantify influence. Community detection algorithms (e.g., Louvain method) will identify clusters and structural holes. This quantitative mapping will be qualitatively enriched through document analysis of policy papers and ethical guidelines to interpret the normative implications of the network structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel empirical foundation for political philosophy and technology ethics, translating Coeckelbergh's relational and democratic concerns into actionable, data-driven diagnostics. It will contribute a methodological framework for anticipatory governance by identifying leverage points for more inclusive and democratic stakeholder engagement in robotics.
proposals.novelty: While SNA is used in innovation studies, its application to explicitly map power for normative, democratic critique in AI ethics is novel. It moves beyond analyzing single entities to reveal the systemic architecture of influence, operationalizing 'relational norms' and 'domination risks' in a concrete analytical model.

ideas.title: Relax the assumption that AI decision-making requires delegation
ideas.description: Design AI systems that augment human judgment without fully autonomous decision-making delegation.
ideas.sourceTitle: 2 SUPERINTELLIGENCE, MONSTERS, AND THE AI APOCALYPSE

ideas.title: Discursive Hermeneutics: Analyzing Engineer Self-Narratives and Artifact Rhetoric
ideas.description: This research applies discourse analysis to the dual texts produced in socio-technical systems: the engineer's first-person reflective accounts (hermeneutic relation) and the official design documents or marketing materials for the same systems (normative anthropomorphism). By analyzing these parallel discourses, the study maps the gaps and consonances between how engineers privately interpret their agency and responsibility in code and how they publicly frame their systems' agency and social role. This reveals the strategic discursive work done to translate technical creation into social narratives, exposing the politics of representation at the human-machine interface.

ideas.title: Stakeholder-Led Speculative Deliberation: Translating Anthropomorphism Provocations into Design Constraints
ideas.description: This research creates speculative design artifacts (e.g., interfaces, product scenarios, service blueprints) that provocatively embody extreme or logical conclusions of current anthropomorphism trends. These artifacts are not merely exhibited but serve as the core material for a series of structured, facilitated deliberation workshops with key stakeholders, including AI engineers, product managers, ethicists, and user advocates. The process systematically maps reactions and debates onto concrete axes of technical and ethical tension, producing a validated set of design principles and potential regulatory or testing benchmarks. The outcome moves beyond critique to generate stakeholder-negotiated, actionable guidance for the responsible design of anthropomorphic systems.

ideas.title: Discourse Analysis of City-Scale Robotic Infrastructure: Unpacking the Politics of Networked Anthropomorphism
ideas.description: This research applies discourse analysis to the planning documents, policy frameworks, and public communications surrounding the deployment of a city-wide network of social robots. It investigates how normative anthropomorphism is strategically scaled and operationalized across an infrastructure, shaping collective human-robot relations for an entire community. The synergy reveals how discourses of efficiency, care, or public safety become materially embedded in the network's architecture, governing interactions at a societal level. This approach critiques the political and ideological dimensions of infrastructural robot design, which neither device-level discourse analysis nor purely technical network studies can achieve alone.

ideas.title: Adapting Bioethical Impact Assessment for Value-Sensitive Relational Design
ideas.description: This idea proposes to adapt the structured, anticipatory assessment frameworks used in biomedical ethics (e.g., ELSI, Anticipatory Governance) to solve the problem of systematically embedding values in relational approaches within HCI. The analogous solution involves integrating ethical analysis prospectively into a technology's lifecycle via stage-gated processes. Adapted to this context, the research would develop a 'Relational Impact Assessment' protocol—a concrete methodological toolkit with phased stages for scoping, stakeholder engagement, value conflict resolution, and iterative audit. This provides a transferable, step-by-step method to ensure critical values are not just identified but actively shape the design and evaluation of relational systems from conception through deployment.

ideas.title: Study the paradox of digital's irrelevance and centrality
ideas.description: Empirically examine the co-existing narratives of digital obsolescence and critical necessity during societal disruption.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Postdigital in Pandemic Times: a Comment on the Covid-19 Crisis and its Political Epistemologies

ideas.title: Applying the Wave-Particle Duality Framework to Technology Philosophy
ideas.description: This research seed expands by analogy to quantum physics' resolution of the wave-particle duality. In that field, the solution was Niels Bohr's principle of complementarity, which accepted both models as complete and valid, their applicability dependent on the experimental context. This directly parallels the need to move beyond the forced binary between naïve instrumentalism (technology as a mere tool) and uncritical posthumanism (technology as an autonomous agent). The adapted idea proposes a 'Complementarity Framework for Technology Philosophy.' Here, instrumentalism and posthumanism are not opposite poles but complementary descriptions. Instrumentalism provides the correct 'local' description of technology within constrained, goal-oriented human projects (akin to a particle). Posthumanism provides the correct 'non-local' description of technology's role in distributed, systemic networks where agency is emergent and hybrid (akin to a wave). The framework's core is that asking which model is 'true' is the wrong question; the critical task is to determine which descriptive model is most appropriate for the specific scale, context, and set of questions being asked about the human-technology relationship.

ideas.title: Mapping Entanglements: A Diagnostic Toolkit for Instrumental-Posthuman Hybrids in Socio-Technical Systems
ideas.description: This research develops a practical, case-driven methodology to analyze how instrumentalist logic (optimization, control) and posthumanist logic (decentering the human, hybrid agency) become inseparably entangled in real-world systems. Instead of treating them as philosophical poles, the toolkit provides heuristics to trace their co-constitution in domains like algorithmic governance, platform ecosystems, or environmental sensing networks. The outcome is a set of diagnostic patterns that reveal where these logics reinforce, subvert, or mutate one another, enabling a more grounded critique of power and agency that moves beyond abstract dualisms.

ideas.title: Power-Explicit Participatory Action Research with Sovereignty Mapping for Social Robotics
ideas.description: This approach rigorously applies PAR methods but begins by formally mapping and negotiating decision-making sovereignty between institutional researchers and the citizen co-researcher cohort. It establishes independent technical translation and advocacy resources for the citizen group to mitigate power asymmetries. The core innovation is a binding 'Power and Exit Protocol' that defines clear mechanisms for citizen-led counter-proposals and a structured exit, ensuring the partnership's integrity and transforming potential tokenism into accountable, power-aware co-design.

ideas.title: Dual problem: Engineer's hermeneutic relation to their code
ideas.description: Study the critical, relational self-understanding of engineers toward their own algorithms and systems.

ideas.title: Intervention & Traceability: A Causal Study of Participatory Mechanisms and Their Material Outcomes in Robotic Design
ideas.description: This research moves beyond descriptive study to causally investigate how specific participatory mechanisms, designed to enact genuine citizen power, influence the technical and ethical outcomes of robot design. Through a comparative, longitudinal analysis of multiple design projects, it measures the traceability of citizen input into functional specifications, safety protocols, and governance models. The study employs action research to iteratively refine participatory tools, ensuring they transfer meaningful agency rather than serving a ceremonial function. The outcome is a validated framework of participatory practices that demonstrably shift design priorities and embed democratic accountability into robotic systems.

ideas.title: Formalizing Social Context as Boundary Conditions for Non-Anthropomorphic Functional Systems
ideas.description: This research investigates the development of robotic systems for social spaces that are strictly non-anthropomorphic in form, interaction, and internal model, yet are rigorously designed against formal models of human social context. The human dimension is not unpacked for emulation but is abstracted into a set of verifiable constraints (e.g., proxemic zones, normative behavior patterns, safety protocols) that act as boundary conditions for system design and validation. The goal is to create robots that are functionally effective in human environments precisely because their operational parameters are defined by, and tested against, explicit models of social context, avoiding both anthropomorphic assumptions and the pitfalls of context-blind design.

ideas.title: Relax assumption that users must become citizens
ideas.description: Study social robotics where user passivity or consumer identity is the designed relational goal.
ideas.sourceTitle: Three Responses to Anthropomorphism in Social Robotics: Towards a Critical, Relational, and Hermeneutic Approach

ideas.title: Social Robotics as Designed Ecosystems: Engineering for Passive Participation
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption in social robotics that users must be actively transformed into engaged 'citizens' of a robotic community. Drawing an analogy from Ecosystem Ecology, where the role of 'passenger species' (as opposed to 'driver' or keystone species) is recognized as valid and functional for ecosystem stability, this study proposes to design social robotics where user passivity or a consumer identity is the intended relational goal. The analogous solution from ecology—which shifted focus from only active participants to modeling the contributions of passive elements to system resilience—is adapted. The research would develop new design frameworks, interaction paradigms, and evaluation metrics that measure success not by user activation or skill acquisition, but by the stability, satisfaction, and functional harmony of a human-robot system where the human occupies a designed, passive consumer niche.

ideas.title: Robotic Counter-Projection: Using Non-Humanoid Form and Behavior to Disrupt Anthropocentric User Modeling
ideas.description: This research seed expands via analogy to psychotherapy techniques for disrupting cognitive projections. The analogous solution involves a therapist deliberately not confirming a client's projected internal model to force schema revision. Adapted to robotics, the idea is to design robots that challenge users' anthropocentric self-conceptions by employing systematically non-humanoid form and behavior. These robots would operate on an alien yet legible logic (e.g., physics-based, ecosystemic), refusing to confirm human-like social projections. This creates a therapeutic-like dissonance, de-anthropomorphizing the interaction and prompting the user to adapt their self-conception to a broader, less human-centered operational reality, scaling the dynamic between user and machine.

ideas.title: Algorithmic Mirror: Applying Clinical Supervision to Engineer-Code Relations
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution of 'clinical supervision' from psychotherapy training to address the dual problem of an engineer's hermeneutic relation to their code. In clinical psychology, therapists engage in supervised reflection on their sessions to understand their own relational biases and impacts (countertransference). The adapted idea proposes 'Technical Supervision' as a parallel, structured reflective practice for engineers. In this practice, engineers would periodically present and dissect their own algorithms and systems with a supervisor or peer group, explicitly analyzing not the functional correctness, but the engineer's own assumptions, values, emotional investments, and perceived responsibilities embedded within the technical artifact. The goal is to formalize a method for scaling critical self-understanding, making the often-implicit hermeneutic relation a subject of deliberate examination to improve ethical, robust, and socially aware system design.

ideas.title: Use actor-network theory to map choreography

ideas.title: Adapt Interpretative Phenomenological Analysis (IPA) from health psychology to HRI
ideas.description: This idea proposes adapting Interpretative Phenomenological Analysis (IPA), a qualitative method from health psychology used to explore how individuals make sense of profound personal experiences like chronic illness, to the study of human-robot interaction (HRI). The analogous solution involves semi-structured interviews focused on personal meaning and a double hermeneutic analytical process. Adapted to HRI, researchers would employ IPA's protocol to conduct in-depth interviews with users about specific, meaningful interactions with social robots (e.g., a first encounter, a moment of perceived empathy, or a breakdown in interaction). The analysis would then proceed through IPA's two-stage interpretive framework: first, understanding the user's personal sense-making of the robotic encounter, and second, interpreting this account through psychological and relational theories. This method would generate rich, idiographic insights into the lived experience of HRI, moving beyond functional usability to explore how humans psychologically integrate robotic agents into their social and emotional worlds.

ideas.title: Triangulated Behavioral Hermeneutics: Mapping the Gap Between Engineers' Stated Understanding and Their Algorithmic Practice
ideas.description: This research systematically investigates the relationship between software engineers' professed understanding of their systems and their actual development behaviors. By combining fine-grained telemetry from programming environments with real-time think-aloud protocols and artifact-cued retrospective interviews, the study creates a three-dimensional map of the engineer-code relationship. This approach reveals discrepancies, blind spots, and tacit knowledge that purely introspective methods would miss. The resulting framework provides a robust, evidence-based account of how hermeneutic relations to code are enacted, contested, and evolved in practice, offering concrete insights for improving system design, documentation, and ethical accountability.

ideas.title: Comparative Historical Sociology of Techno-Moral Orders: Unpacking Co-constitution through Paired Practice Regimes
ideas.description: This research systematically compares how distinct, contemporaneous communities of practice generated different 'techno-moral orders'—integrated systems of material routines, tools, and normative justifications. By analyzing paired historical cases (e.g., early modern laboratories and accounting houses, or 19th-century factories and reform schools), it empirically traces the co-constitution of specific technologies and virtues within concrete social and material settings. The approach moves beyond monolithic narratives to reveal how virtues are enacted, contested, and stabilized through daily engagement with artifacts, resisting deterministic claims about technology's influence. The outcome is a framework for understanding moral pluralism as embedded in divergent socio-technical systems.

ideas.title: Scale across cultural interpretations of anthropomorphism

ideas.title: Robots as Participants in Autopoietic Ecological Loops: A Non-Instrumental Framework
ideas.description: This research designs robotic systems that are not instruments *for* human interaction with nature, but operational participants *within* specific ecological or animal communication loops. A robot's core imperative is to maintain its own operational continuity (e.g., energy, structural integrity) by performing actions that are measured, by embedded algorithms, to correlate with or enhance the autopoietic resilience of the target loop—such as a forest nutrient cycle or a herd's spatial-temporal dynamics. The design explicitly forbows the extraction of data or achievement of human-defined goals as a primary driver, instead using real-time, on-board analysis of ecological signals to guide minimal, reciprocal action. The outcome is a critique of instrumentalism through a practical demonstration of a robotics that seeks viability *from within* a system, not utility *from outside* it.

ideas.title: The Value Impact Assessment Protocol: Closing the Accountability Loop in Value-Sensitive System Design
ideas.description: This research develops and validates a Value Impact Assessment (VIA) protocol that operationalizes Value-Sensitive Design principles into an accountable, closed-loop engineering process. The protocol requires designers to formally specify verifiable metrics for how critical values (e.g., fairness, autonomy, dignity) will be manifested and measured in system outcomes, moving beyond conceptual embedding. It then institutes mandatory, periodic post-deployment audits against these metrics, combining algorithmic auditing with stakeholder impact interviews. The results of these audits trigger predefined system modification procedures, creating a concrete mechanism for value alignment throughout the system lifecycle and preventing the symbolic adoption of ethical frameworks.

ideas.title: Scale down: Epistemic agency in local governance

ideas.title: Ethnography-Grounded Co-Design: An Iterative Loop for Generating and Testing HRI Principles
ideas.description: This approach embeds ethnographic study within a rigorous, iterative design science framework. Researchers first conduct immersive fieldwork to document HRI as embodied social practice, then rapidly translate key observations into low-fidelity interaction prototypes. These prototypes are tested and refined through a series of facilitated co-design workshops with the studied user community, deliberately probing the boundaries and transferability of the observed practices. The final output is a set of contextualized, yet abstractable, design principles and interaction patterns that are both empirically grounded and directly actionable for robot development.

ideas.title: Hermeneutic Micro-Interaction Analysis for Foundational Robot Design
ideas.description: This research applies hermeneutic interpretation techniques to the participatory design and analysis of brief, task-focused interactions with minimalist, non-humanoid robots. By focusing on micro-interactions, the complexity of the robot is minimized, allowing the hermeneutic process to isolate and magnify the core relational dynamics, meanings, and assumptions co-constructed by users and engineers. The resulting insights generate a formalized vocabulary of foundational interaction primitives—such as 'responsive delay,' 'directional ambiguity,' or 'taskful delegation'—that are stripped of anthropomorphic bias. This vocabulary then serves as a rigorous, interpretively-grounded toolkit for designing more complex robotic systems, ensuring their relational foundations are consciously built from well-understood, human-centric components.

ideas.title: Bio-Inspired Robotics: Designing Non-Anthropomorphic Environmental Probes
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that robots are primarily instruments within human social or industrial systems. Drawing an analogy from marine biology, where deep-sea exploration tools are designed to be non-invasive elements of the environment they study, we propose designing robots as instruments primarily for environmental or animal interaction. The analogous solution involved creating observation tools that mimic environmental features or benign fauna to minimize disruption. Adapted to robotics, this means developing robots with non-anthropomorphic, environmentally-consonant morphologies (e.g., soft, slow-moving, or current-driven forms) and behaviors calibrated not for human efficiency but for maximal integration and minimal disturbance within the target non-human system. The research would focus on creating a new instrumental paradigm where success is measured by the quality and authenticity of the robot's interaction with its environmental or animal context, rather than its performance of a human-defined task.

ideas.title: Collaborative Scaffolding: Using Non-Anthropomorphic Robot Partners to Expand User Capability Models
ideas.description: This research designs robot partners that perform collaborative tasks (e.g., creative design, environmental mapping, problem-solving) by leveraging distinctly non-human morphologies and sensing modalities, such as 360-degree perception or swarm coordination. The interaction is structured so that success depends on the user integrating these alien capabilities into their own workflow, creating a functional dependency and appreciation. Post-task guided reflection then helps users consciously articulate how their own human capabilities were complemented and extended by the non-human partner, fostering an expanded, less anthropocentric model of agency and partnership.

ideas.title: Quantifying Relational Depth via Adaptive Perturbation in Long-Term Human-Robot Interaction
ideas.description: This research investigates the robustness and depth of human relational models formed with social robots by introducing controlled, algorithmic perturbations into the robot's interaction style over extended periods. Using a longitudinal, multi-group design, it compares interactions with an adaptively shifting robot against a static control, measuring behavioral, subjective, and physiological responses to these changes. The core aim is to distinguish between superficial behavioral adaptation and a more profound, flexible relational understanding that can accommodate and recalibrate to the partner's evolution. This method provides a rigorous, falsifiable framework for assessing the quality, not just the persistence, of long-term human-robot relationships.

ideas.title: Cultural Anthropomorphism Scaling via Ecological Niche Modeling
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from ecology's 'ecological niche modeling' (ENM) to scale the relational approach to anthropomorphism across diverse cultural contexts. In ENM, a species' fundamental niche is defined by core environmental variables, which are then used to predict its distribution in new ecosystems based on local conditions. Analogously, this study will first deconstruct the relational approach into its fundamental, abstract variables (e.g., perceived autonomy, capacity for social exchange). These variables form the core 'niche' of anthropomorphic relation. Researchers will then identify key cultural-normative 'environmental parameters' (e.g., social hierarchy norms, ontological beliefs about objects) across a wide spectrum of societies. The adapted solution is to build a predictive, scalable model that maps how the interaction between these two sets of variables—the core relational parameters and the local cultural conditions—determines the specific manifestation, acceptance, or rejection of anthropomorphism in human-artifact interactions. This allows for systematic, testable predictions about anthropomorphic interpretations in any cultural context, scaling the relational approach beyond isolated case studies.

ideas.title: A Paleoecological Approach to Technological Practice: Stratigraphic Analysis of Virtue Formation
ideas.description: This research adapts the stratigraphic analytical method from paleoecology to historically analyze how technologies shape virtues through practice. In paleoecology, scientists analyze layered physical evidence (like pollen cores) to reconstruct past ecosystems and systemic changes. The adapted approach treats historical periods as strata, examining the cumulative, layered record of technological practices—encompassing tools, skills, routines, and their associated discourses—to reconstruct the formation and transformation of virtues (e.g., patience, precision, communal responsibility). By performing comparative 'core samples' across different technological eras (e.g., artisan workshops, industrial factories, digital platforms), the research aims to map systemic shifts in virtue ecosystems, moving beyond isolated case studies to reveal long-term, structural interactions between material practice and moral character.

ideas.title: Applying Archaeological Stratigraphy Analysis to Robot Design Workshop Interpretation
ideas.description: This research adapts archaeological stratigraphy analysis methods to address hermeneutic interpretation challenges in participatory robot design workshops. In archaeology, stratigraphic analysis is used to interpret complex, multi-layered historical sites by systematically documenting each layer's context and relationships to reconstruct chronological sequences and distinguish between different occupation phases. Similarly, in robot design workshops involving users, engineers, and other stakeholders, each participant group brings distinct interpretive 'layers' with their own assumptions, values, and technical understandings. The adapted approach would develop a systematic framework for documenting these interpretive layers as they emerge during design sessions, analyzing how they interact, merge, or conflict, and tracing the evolution of design concepts through these layered interpretations. This would provide designers with a clearer hermeneutic map of the participatory process, helping identify points of interpretive convergence and divergence that might otherwise remain obscured in complex workshop dynamics.

ideas.title: Culturally-grounded meta-analysis of relationship formation with human-like artifacts
ideas.description: This research begins with longitudinal, participatory fieldwork in diverse cultural contexts to collaboratively elucidate local ontologies and vernacular categories for human-like artifacts, avoiding the imposition of external frameworks. The core analysis then compares the processes, conditions, and moral logics of relationship formation that emerge from these distinct grounded theories. The outcome is a meta-theoretical model of cross-cultural variation in human-artifact relationality, rooted in demonstrated empirical differences rather than a priori assumptions.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Succession Models to Long-Term Human-Robot Relationship Dynamics
ideas.description: This research proposes to study the long-term evolution of critical, relational understanding in human-robot interaction by drawing an analogy from ecology's study of ecological succession. In ecology, succession describes the predictable sequence of change in species composition and community structure in an ecosystem over extended periods. Ecologists solve the challenge of studying these slow processes through longitudinal plot studies and chronosequence methods (comparing sites of different ages). Adapted to HRI, the research would conceptualize the human-robot relational landscape as an ecosystem that undergoes succession from initial encounter through maturation. The methodology would combine a true longitudinal study, tracking a cohort of human-robot dyads with repeated, multi-modal measurements over years, with a cross-sectional chronosequence component comparing relationships of varying durations (e.g., 1 month, 1 year, 5 years) to model the trajectory of relational understanding, identifying critical transition points, stable states, and potential relational 'climaxes' or degradations.

ideas.title: Probing Habit Resilience in HRI Through Controlled Context Disruption
ideas.description: This research employs longitudinal studies of human-robot interaction, but crucially incorporates planned, controlled disruptions to the interaction context to measure habit resilience, not just formation. By using a crossover design that alternates between stable and altered environmental or robot behavioral cues, it directly tests for the automaticity and context-dependence of the developed routines. This method distinguishes fragile, context-bound patterns from robust habits, overcoming the measurement validity problem posed by the habit discontinuity effect. The resulting data provides a more rigorous, multi-dimensional map of how true habits form and stabilize in HRI.

ideas.title: Triangulated Phenomenology: Integrating Synchronized Multi-Modal Capture with Cued-Recall Interviews for HRI
ideas.description: This method combines the depth of phenomenological inquiry with the precision of behavioral and physiological measurement to study lived experience in HRI. During an interaction, multi-modal data (video, gaze, physiology, robot logs) is continuously recorded and synchronized. In the subsequent phenomenological interview, segments of this recording are replayed to the participant to cue detailed recall of their moment-to-moment experience, anchoring their reflections in concrete, observable events. This triangulation produces a richer, more robust account that mitigates the flaws of pure introspection by linking subjective meaning to objective traces of the interaction.

ideas.title: Minimalist Robot Interaction Protocols via Molecular Signaling Analogy
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from molecular biology, where complex cellular behaviors emerge from simple, rule-based molecular interactions, to the domain of minimalist robotics. The analogous solution involves modeling discrete molecular signaling events—characterized by specificity, transient binding, and triggered responses—as a framework for robot micro-interactions. The adapted idea is to design minimalist, non-humanoid robots with a limited set of broadcastable 'signal' behaviors (e.g., a specific light pattern, sound pulse, or magnetic pulse) and corresponding 'receptor' logic to detect and respond to those signals. This creates a relational approach where brief, task-focused interactions (like passing an object, aligning for a connection, or clearing a path) are governed by simple, scalable rules akin to biochemical affinities, enabling coordinated group behavior without centralized control or complex individual cognition.
